title: Fermentation Tools & Technology Through History
subtitle: From Neolithic clay pots to modern airlocks - 9,000 years of fermentation vessels

image.caption: Korean onggi pots - breathable earthenware vessels that have been used for kimchi fermentation for centuries

sections.content: The history of fermentation is inseparable from the history of the vessels, weights, and tools used to make it happen. Long before anyone understood microbiology, humans discovered that certain containers - sealed clay pots, stone-weighted crocks, buried earthenware jars - produced reliably preserved, delicious food [cite:c1]. The evolution of fermentation technology is a story of material science, cultural exchange, and the slow, practical discovery of what we now understand as anaerobic conditions, selective pressure, and pH management [[article:how-lacto-fermentation-works]]. From the Neolithic clay jars of Jiahu to smartphone-connected fermentation trackers, each innovation reflects both the science of its time and the enduring human need to preserve food safely.
The Earliest Evidence: Jiahu, China (c. 7000 BCE)

The oldest confirmed chemical evidence of fermentation comes from the Neolithic village of Jiahu in Henan Province, China, dating to approximately 7000 BCE. Archaeochemist Patrick McGovern and his team at the University of Pennsylvania Museum analysed residues inside pottery jars and identified tartaric acid, beeswax, and other compounds consistent with a fermented beverage made from rice, honey, and hawthorn fruit [cite:c2]. These jars - simple, thick-walled earthenware vessels - represent the earliest known fermentation technology. They were not purpose-built for fermentation; they were general-purpose storage vessels that happened to create the semi-anaerobic, stable-temperature conditions needed for microbial transformation. The significance of the Jiahu discovery is that fermentation appears to have been among humanity's earliest food technologies, predating bread-making and possibly even deliberate agriculture.
Mesopotamia and Egypt: Beer, Bread, and Purpose-Built Vessels

By 3000–4000 BCE, Mesopotamian and Egyptian civilisations had developed dedicated fermentation vessels. Mesopotamian brewers used large clay vats called namzitu for beer production, with perforated inner vessels that served as primitive strainers. The Hymn to Ninkasi, a Sumerian poem dating to approximately 1800 BCE, describes the beer-making process in detail, including the specific vessels used at each stage [cite:c1]. Egyptian tomb paintings from the same era depict bread-making and brewing side by side, reflecting the close relationship between these two fermentation processes. The Egyptians developed tall, narrow amphora-like jars for storing fermented beverages - their tapered shape allowed sediment to collect at the bottom while clear liquid could be decanted from above. This was an early, intuitive solution to the problem of separating fermented product from spent yeast and bacterial residue.
Roman Dolia and the Fish Sauce Amphorae

The Romans elevated fermentation vessel design to an industrial scale. Dolia - massive earthenware vessels holding 1,000 to 3,000 litres - were used for fermenting wine and were often buried up to their rims in the ground. The burial served a critical function: it provided thermal insulation, maintaining a more stable temperature during fermentation than above-ground storage could offer [[article:temperature-and-fermentation]]. Roman garum and liquamen - fermented fish sauces that were the ketchup of the ancient Mediterranean - were produced in dedicated facilities (cetariae) using large stone or clay vats. Archaeologists have excavated garum production sites at Pompeii, Baelo Claudia in Spain, and Lixus in Morocco, revealing standardised amphorae used for shipping the finished product across the empire. The Romans also developed the concept of terroir in fermentation: different production sites were known for distinct flavour profiles, much as wine regions are today [[article:fermentation-traditions-worldwide]].
Korean Onggi: The Breathing Vessel

Korean onggi pots represent one of the most sophisticated traditional fermentation technologies ever developed. These earthenware vessels, used for centuries to ferment kimchi, doenjang (soybean paste), ganjang (soy sauce), and gochujang (chilli paste), have a unique property: they are microporous, allowing slow gas exchange while preventing liquid loss and contamination [cite:c1]. The clay body of onggi is mixed with fine sand and fired at relatively low temperatures (around 800–900°C), creating a network of microscopic pores. This controlled permeability allows carbon dioxide produced during fermentation to escape gradually [[article:role-of-carbon-dioxide]] while permitting just enough oxygen exchange to support the complex microbial succession that gives Korean fermented foods their distinctive character. Traditional Korean households maintained a jangdokdae - an outdoor platform or terrace where dozens of onggi pots stood, each containing a different ferment at a different stage of maturity.

sections.title: Why Onggi Is Hard to Replicate
sections.content: Modern attempts to reproduce onggi's properties with other materials have largely failed. The specific combination of clay composition, sand grain size, firing temperature, and traditional glazing (using natural ash glaze on the exterior only) creates a breathability that glass, plastic, and standard stoneware cannot match. If you can source authentic onggi, it is worth the investment for long-term ferments like doenjang or gochujang. For shorter vegetable ferments like kimchi or kkakdugi [[recipe:kkakdugi-radish]], glass jars work perfectly well.
Japanese Tsukemono Stones and Wooden Presses

Japanese pickle-making (tsukemono) developed a distinctive set of tools centred on the tsukemono-ishi - a heavy pressing stone used to compress vegetables in a container, expelling air and liquid simultaneously. The traditional setup consists of a wooden or ceramic barrel (taru), the vegetables layered with salt and seasonings, a wooden pressing lid (otoshibuta) that fits inside the barrel, and a heavy stone placed on top. The weight of the stone serves multiple functions: it forces water out of the vegetables by mechanical pressure (complementing the osmotic action of salt [[article:salt-and-fermentation]]), it keeps the vegetables submerged below the brine, and it compresses the mass to reduce air pockets. Japanese fermenters developed an intuitive understanding of the relationship between pressing weight and fermentation speed - heavier stones produce faster but sometimes less nuanced results.
European Stoneware Crocks: The Water-Seal Revolution

The water-sealed stoneware fermentation crock, most associated with German and Central European sauerkraut production [[recipe:sauerkraut-classic]], represents a genuine breakthrough in fermentation vessel design. These crocks feature a channel (gutter) around the rim that is filled with water. The lid sits in this channel, creating a one-way valve: carbon dioxide produced during fermentation can bubble out through the water, but air cannot enter. This simple mechanism solved the two biggest problems of open-vessel fermentation - mould growth on the surface and exposure to oxygen [cite:c3]. The Harsch crock, a German design, and the Ohio Stoneware crock, its American counterpart, became standards in home fermentation. These crocks are typically made from vitrified (non-porous) stoneware, fired at high temperatures (1200–1300°C), and glazed on the interior to prevent absorption of flavours and acids. Many come with matching stoneware weights that fit perfectly inside the crock to keep vegetables submerged.
The Mason Jar Revolution

John Landis Mason's 1858 patent for the screw-threaded glass jar transformed home food preservation - first for canning, and later for fermentation. The mason jar (and its cousins the Ball jar, Kilner jar, and Weck jar) democratised small-batch fermentation by providing a cheap, reusable, transparent, non-reactive vessel available in standard sizes [cite:c1]. Transparency was a genuine innovation: for the first time, fermenters could observe their ferments without opening the container - watching bubble activity, brine clarity, colour changes, and the rise and fall of vegetables. The two-piece lid design (flat disc and threaded ring) can be loosely applied to allow gas escape, or combined with aftermarket airlock inserts for a proper one-way valve. The mason jar's main limitations are its relatively small volume (typically 500 mL to 2 litres), its narrow mouth (which makes packing whole vegetables difficult), and the lack of a built-in weight system to keep vegetables submerged.
Modern Airlocks, Valves, and Lid Systems

The past two decades have seen an explosion of purpose-built fermentation hardware. Silicone airlock lids that fit standard mason jars (such as those made by Masontops, Kraut Source, and similar brands) provide one-way gas release without the need for a water-filled channel. Spring-loaded glass or silicone fermentation weights keep vegetables submerged. Dedicated fermentation crocks with integrated airlocks and weights offer all-in-one solutions. The Fido-style (bail-wire) jar, with its rubber gasket, naturally burps excess pressure while maintaining a reasonable seal. For larger-scale fermentation, food-grade plastic buckets with drilled lids and standard homebrew airlocks (the S-shaped water-filled type) offer an economical solution. The key principle behind all of these devices remains the same one that Korean onggi and European water-sealed crocks discovered centuries ago: allow CO₂ out, keep oxygen (and contaminants) from getting in [[article:role-of-carbon-dioxide]].
Vacuum Sealing and Fermentation

Vacuum-sealed bags offer a modern, space-efficient approach to fermentation that has gained popularity among both home fermenters and professional chefs. The technique is simple: salted vegetables are placed in a vacuum bag, the air is removed with a vacuum sealer, and the bag is sealed. Fermentation proceeds anaerobically within the bag, with CO₂ gradually inflating it. The bag must be burped periodically or a small corner can be left slightly unsealed to allow gas to escape. Advantages include perfect anaerobic conditions from the start (no need for weights or brine submersion), minimal brine required, compact storage (bags can be stacked flat), and easy labelling. Disadvantages include the environmental cost of single-use plastic, the inability to taste during fermentation without breaking the seal, and the risk of bag rupture if CO₂ pressure builds too quickly. This method works exceptionally well for sauerkraut [[recipe:sauerkraut-classic]], curtido [[recipe:curtido-salvadoran]], and any shredded vegetable ferment.
The Science of Vessel Material
The material of your fermentation vessel interacts with the fermentation process in ways that matter. Understanding these interactions helps you choose the right vessel for each ferment [cite:c3].

rows: Traditional barrel ferments, wine, soy sauce
AVOID - reacts with lactic acid, can leach metals
AVOID - iron leaches into acidic ferments

sections.title: Avoid Reactive Metals
sections.content: Never ferment in copper, brass, aluminium, or uncoated cast iron vessels. Lactic acid is corrosive to these metals and will leach metallic compounds into your food, causing off-flavours and potential health risks. Stainless steel (specifically grades 304 and 316, also known as 18/8 and 18/10) is the only metal safe for fermentation. If you are unsure about a vessel's material, test it with a magnet - food-grade stainless steel is weakly magnetic or non-magnetic.
Fermentation Weights Through the Ages

Keeping food submerged below the brine is one of the oldest and most essential techniques in fermentation. Throughout history, fermenters have used an astonishing variety of weights for this purpose. River stones and smooth rocks were the earliest weights - still perfectly effective today if scrubbed and boiled before use. Japanese tsukemono stones were purpose-selected heavy river stones, sometimes carved to fit specific containers. European sauerkraut crocks came with fitted ceramic or stoneware discs. Cabbage cores, water-filled zip-lock bags (which conform to the jar's shape), small plates weighted with a jar of water, glass fermentation weights (designed to fit wide-mouth mason jars), and even cleaned, boiled stones from the garden - all serve the same fundamental purpose: maintaining anaerobic conditions by keeping the food below the brine surface [[article:how-lacto-fermentation-works]].
Timeline: 9,000 Years of Fermentation Technology

sections.content: The Future: Smart Fermentation and Connected Devices

The latest frontier in fermentation technology is the integration of sensors and data logging. Several companies and open-source projects have developed devices that sit inside or on top of fermentation vessels and continuously monitor pH [[article:ph-levels-fermentation]], temperature [[article:temperature-and-fermentation]], and CO₂ production, transmitting data to a smartphone app. These tools can alert you when your ferment reaches a target pH, when temperature drifts outside an optimal range, or when CO₂ production slows (indicating fermentation is tapering off). While experienced fermenters can judge these things by taste, smell, and observation, smart trackers offer a learning tool for beginners and a quality-control tool for anyone producing ferments at scale. The fundamental principles, however, have not changed since Jiahu: create the right environment, provide the right substrate, and let the microorganisms do the work [cite:c1].
Choosing the Right Vessel for Your Ferment

For beginners, a wide-mouth glass mason jar (1 litre) with a loose-fitting lid or aftermarket airlock is the most versatile and affordable starting point. It works for sauerkraut [[recipe:sauerkraut-classic]], brine pickles [[recipe:brine-pickles-basic]], fermented salsa [[recipe:fermented-salsa]], and virtually any small-batch vegetable ferment. As you scale up, a 5-litre water-sealed stoneware crock is ideal for sauerkraut and other shredded vegetable ferments. For Indian-style pickles that benefit from some sun exposure, traditional ceramic or glass jars with loose cloth covers work well. If space is limited, vacuum bag fermentation is worth learning. Whatever you choose, the non-negotiable requirements are: food-safe material, ability to maintain anaerobic conditions (or near-anaerobic), and a way to keep the food submerged below the brine [[article:troubleshooting-ferments]].

items: **Beginner**: Wide-mouth glass mason jar (1L) + glass weight + airlock lid or loose standard lid
**Intermediate**: Water-sealed stoneware crock (3–5L) with matching weights for sauerkraut and large batches
**Space-conscious**: Vacuum bags with a channel vacuum sealer for flat, stackable ferments
**Traditional Korean**: Onggi pot for kimchi, doenjang, and other long-aged ferments
**Large scale**: Food-grade HDPE bucket (10–20L) with drilled lid and S-shaped airlock
**Professional**: Stainless steel vessels with temperature control, sampling ports, and integrated sensors

sections.content: Cleaning and Maintaining Fermentation Equipment

Fermentation equipment does not need to be sterilised - you are not brewing beer or making wine where specific yeast strains must dominate from the start. Clean vessels are sufficient [cite:c1]. Wash glass and ceramic vessels with hot water and dish soap, rinse thoroughly, and allow to air dry. Avoid antibacterial soaps, which can leave residues that inhibit LAB. Stoneware crocks should not be subjected to thermal shock (do not pour boiling water into a cold crock). Wooden tools (tampers, spoons) should be scrubbed and air-dried between uses; over time they will develop a patina of beneficial microorganisms. Silicone airlock lids and gaskets should be checked periodically for tears or degradation. The most important maintenance habit is thorough rinsing - soap residue is a more common cause of fermentation problems than inadequate cleaning.

citations.text: Katz, Sandor Ellix. The Art of Fermentation: An In-Depth Exploration of Essential Concepts and Processes from Around the World. Chelsea Green Publishing, 2012.
McGovern, Patrick E. et al. Fermented beverages of pre- and proto-historic China. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 101(51), 17593–17598, 2004.
Shockey, Kirsten & Christopher. Fermented Vegetables: Creative Recipes for Fermenting 64 Vegetables & Herbs in Krauts, Kimchis, Brined Pickles, Chutneys, Relishes & Pastes. Storey Publishing, 2014.
